w:r.w:t: Got it. We are staying purely on the Frontend. We are building the
as its own completely standalone, pristine application.

. It is strictly a beautiful, interactive "dumb terminal." It renders your 2D tiles, draws your stamina bars, captures your mouse clicks, and sends them as JSON payloads to whatever backend you eventually plug it into.

If you are starting fresh, ditch the old auto-coder repository completely. Create a new React project (e.g., saga_vtt_client). Here is the exact, developer-ready blueprint to build the 5-panel layout we discussed.
🎨 S.A.G.A. VTT BLUEPRINT: The Player Interface
Keep your components strictly organized by the visual zones of the screen.
│ │ └── useGameStore.ts # Zustand (or Context) to hold the JSON state
│ │ │ └── DirectorLog.tsx # The AI Chat & Math Feed
│ │ │ └── ResourceOrbs.tsx # Stamina / Focus bars & Burn Slider
to lock the interface into a fullscreen, non-scrolling "Video Game" layout. No gross scrollbars. Everything is fixed to the screen edges.
<div className="w-screen h-screen overflow-hidden bg-zinc-950 text-white flex flex-col font-sans">
<div className="w-96 bg-zinc-900/90 border-r border-zinc-800 flex flex-col z-10">
{/* CENTER: The Map (Fills all remaining space) */}
{/* RIGHT DRAWER: The Bio-Matrix (Slides in and out) */}
<div className="w-1/3 max-w-md bg-zinc-900 border-l border-zinc-800 shadow-2xl z-30 p-4 overflow-y-auto">
className="absolute top-4 right-4 z-40 bg-zinc-800 p-2 rounded hover:bg-zinc-700"
{/* BOTTOM SECTION: The Action Deck (Fixed Height) */}
<div className="h-48 bg-zinc-950 border-t border-zinc-800 z-20 flex justify-center items-center shadow-[0_-10px_40px_rgba(0,0,0,0.5)]">
This is the most important tactical component. It visualizes your strict
rules, along with the "Burn Slider" for Tactical Grit.
// In a real app, these come from your JSON State Store
<div className="flex w-full max-w-6xl h-full items-end pb-4 px-6 gap-8">
<div className="w-24 h-24 rounded-full border-4 border-zinc-800 bg-zinc-900 relative overflow-hidden shadow-[0_0_20px_rgba(239,68,68,0.2)]">
className="absolute bottom-0 w-full bg-red-600 transition-all duration-500"
<div className="absolute inset-0 flex items-center justify-center font-bold text-2xl text-white drop-shadow-md">
<div className="flex items-center gap-4 bg-zinc-900/50 p-2 rounded-lg border border-zinc-800">
<button key={skill} className="px-6 py-4 bg-zinc-800 hover:bg-red-900 border-b-4 border-zinc-950 rounded text-sm font-bold uppercase tracking-wider transition-colors">
<div key={slot} className="w-16 h-16 bg-zinc-900 border-2 border-zinc-700 rounded-md flex items-center justify-center hover:border-zinc-500 cursor-pointer">
4. The Data Contract (How it stays standalone)
To keep this UI completely decoupled from the AI and the backend, you define a single "State Interface" (e.g., in useGameStore.ts).
// If true, disable all buttons (Director is talking)
How the Interaction Works (The Standard Cycle):
roll dice. It fires an event:
The UI immediately sets ui_locked = true so you can't spam click.
Two seconds later, the backend replies with a fresh ClientGameState JSON object.
React receives the JSON, updates the Stamina bar (which visually drops down), adds the AI's story to the chat_log, and unlocks the UI.

This React foundation perfectly isolates your front-end. You can spend weeks making the CSS look incredible, wiring up the PixiJS MapCanvas to render your 2D tiles, and building the Bio-Matrix slider without ever worrying about the backend Python math.
Does this clean, pure-frontend React architecture give you the exact starting point you need to build the player view?
